coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of silver nanoparticles/carbon dots for a charge transfer study and peroxidase-like catalytic monitoring by surface-enhanced Raman scattering
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Hongyue
Guo, Yue
Zhu, Shoujun
Song, Yubin
Jin, Jing
Ji, Wei
Song, Wei
Zhao, Bing
Yang, Bai
Ozaki, Yukihiro
coredata.dc:description: In this work, carbon dots (CDs) were combined with Ag nanoparticles (NPs) by a chemical reaction to form Ag NPs/CDs hybrid, which was then used as a novel surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) substrate. During the synthetic process, carboxyl groups on the surface of Ag NPs were reacted with amino groups of CDs in an amidation reaction. The D and G bands of CDs in the Ag NPs/CDs hybrid could be easily detected by SERS. By employing p-aminothiophenol (PATP) molecules as SERS probes, the Ag NPs/CDs hybrid substrate could detect PATP in diluted solutions of concentration as low as 10−9 M. The charge transfer (CT) effect on SERS spectra with different excitation wavelengths in the prepared Ag NPs/CDs hybrid and PATP system was also investigated. It was found that addition of CDs changes the degree of CT between Ag NPs and PATP molecules. Since the prepared Ag NPs/CDs hybrid also showed a peroxidase-like activity toward the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine using H2O2, which can provide the sensitive detection of H2O2 by SERS technique.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ag nanoparticles
Surface enhanced raman scattering
Charge transfer effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and morphology of Ag NPs/CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the fabrication process of Ag NPs/CDs.
(a) A TEM image of Ag NPs/CDs. The inset shows an enlarged TEM image of the prepared Ag NPs/CDs. (b) A XRD pattern of Ag NPs/CDs.
(a) UV–vis and (b) fluorescence spectra of Ag NPs/CDs, Ag NPs and CDs.
XPS spectra of Ag NPs/CDs. (a) Ag 3d region. (b) C 1s region. (c) N 1s region. (d) O 1s region.
The Raman spectra of Ag NPs/CDs, CDs and Ag NPs.
SERS spectra of PATP with different concentrations (10−5, 10−6, 10−7, 10−8 and 10−9 M) using Ag NPs/CDs with the excitation wavelength of 633nm.
SERS spectra of PATP molecules with 514.5 (a and a’), 532 (b and b’), 633 (c and c’) and 785nm (d and d’).
The SERS intensity ratio of two bands at 1144 and 1075cm−1 with different excitation energies (excitation wavelengths).

(a) Time dependent SERS spectra of PATP molecules using Ag NPs/CDs. (b) The relative intensity of two lands at 1144 (b2 mode) and 1075 (a1 mode) cm−1 versus time with different powers. (c) The ratio versus power at 300s.
SERS spectra of oxidized TMB molecules by H2O2 using Ag NPs/CDs substrate, TMB molecules without H2O2 and DMSO, respectively.
SERS spectra of TMB molecules in the presence of H2O2 with different concentrations 10−2, 10−3, 10−4, 10−5, 10−6, 10−7 and 10−8 M using Ag NPs/CDs as both catalyst and SERS substrate.

SERS intensity of the oxidized TMB molecules at 1610cm−1 versus the concentration of hydrogen peroxide using Ag NPs/CDs as substrates. The inset displays the liner variation with the 10−5–10−8 M concentration range of H2O2.
Wavenumber and assignment of bands in the SERS spectrum of PATP molecular with Ag NPs/CDs (633nm excitation).

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of silver nanoparticles/carbon dots for a charge transfer study and peroxidase-like catalytic monitoring by surface-enhanced Raman scattering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science and Technology, Kwansei Gakuin University, 2-1 Gakuen, Sanda, Hyogo 669-1337, Japan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116023, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Silver nanoparticles/carbon dots have been fabricated via a chemical reaction strategy and used as an efficient SERS substrate for a charge transfer study and peroxidase-like catalysis monitoring.

In this work, carbon dots (CDs) were combined with Ag nanoparticles (NPs) by a chemical reaction to form Ag NPs/CDs hybrid, which was then used as a novel surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) substrate. During the synthetic process, carboxyl groups on the surface of Ag NPs were reacted with amino groups of CDs in an amidation reaction. The D and G bands of CDs in the Ag NPs/CDs hybrid could be easily detected by SERS. By employing p-aminothiophenol (PATP) molecules as SERS probes, the Ag NPs/CDs hybrid substrate could detect PATP in diluted solutions of concentration as low as 10−9 M. The charge transfer (CT) effect on SERS spectra with different excitation wavelengths in the prepared Ag NPs/CDs hybrid and PATP system was also investigated. It was found that addition of CDs changes the degree of CT between Ag NPs and PATP molecules. Since the prepared Ag NPs/CDs hybrid also showed a peroxidase-like activity toward the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine using H2O2, which can provide the sensitive detection of H2O2 by SERS technique.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are novel, environment-friendly carbon materials, that have become a hot topic in research since their discovery in 2004 due to their unique chemical and physical properties [1]. The term CDs implies that their size is on a scale of a few nanometers, and includes polymer dots, carbon nano-dots, and graphene quantum dots [2]. CDs display some unique properties such as enhanced fluorescence intensities, excellent light stability, and controlled excitation and emission wavelength. Compared to metal quantum dots and fluorescent nanoparticles, CDs also exhibit low-toxicity, good biocompatibility, and small molecular weights and sizes [3–7]. In recent years, they have been widely used in bioimaging [8,9], optoelectronic devices [10,11], pollutant detection [12], and catalysis [11,13]. A variety of strategies including laser ablation [6,8], electrochemical oxidation [10,14,15], hydrothermal reaction [16,17], and microwave-assisted pyrolysis [18,19] have been used to prepare CDs. Recently, the synthesis of CDs-based hybrids has generated significant interest due to their improved properties and enhanced performance in catalysis, photovoltaic devices, and solar hydrogen production [20–22].

Surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) is a highly sensitive technique that may be used for structural characterization and monitoring of analytes [23,24]. Noble metals such as Au, Ag, and Cu have been used as SERS substrates to enhance Raman signals from analytes [25,26]. There are two types of original interaction mechanisms for explaining the signal enhancement by SERS: the electromagnetic (EM) mechanism and the charge transfer (CT) mechanism [27,28]. The EM mechanism is mainly caused by surface plasmon resonance (SPR) taking place on the surface of metal substrates; while the latter involves CT interactions between the adsorbed molecules and metal substrates. In recent years, noble metal-based hybrid nanomaterials have also been reported as efficient SERS substrates [29–31]. These hybrid nanomaterial-based SERS substrates exhibit multifunctional properties, and have even been applied to catalysis and biotechnology [32–34]. For example, SERS substrates that display photocatalytic activity provide the opportunity for real-time, in-situ monitoring of the photocatalytic reaction. Shao and Kang et al. have reported the fabrication of a hybrid system consisting of reduced graphene oxide (rGO), ZnO nanorods arrays and Au nanoparticles, which can be used as a photocatalyst as well as a SERS substrate. The unique properties of this hybrid system enable the in situ detection of organic pollutants by SERS spectra during the photocatalytic degradation process [32]. Recently, our group has demonstrated the preparation of ternary rGO/CuS/Au hybrid nanomaterials that have been employed as a SERS substrate and a peroxidase-like nanocatalyst [34]. This hybrid nanomaterial shows unique catalytic activity in the oxidation of the peroxidase substrate 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) using H2O2 as oxidant. The oxidized TMB product formed by the peroxidase-like catalytic reaction is captured and detected by SERS, thereby making the in situ monitoring of the catalytic process possible [34].

In recent years, many studies have been reported on the preparation and application of CDs. However, there have been few reports on the fabrication of CDs-based hybrid nanomaterials with a unique structure to be applied as SERS substrates for the monitoring of catalytic processes. Our group has prepared core-shell structured Ag@CDs NPs by a one-pot direct reduction of AgNO3 by using CDs as the reducing agent as well as the stabilizer. The prepared Ag@CDs NPs can be used as a SERS substrate and as a nanocatalyst for the in situ monitoring of typical catalytic processes [35]. To improve the interactions between Ag NPs and CDs, in this study, a simple synthetic approach was employed for the preparation of Ag NPs/CDs hybrid utilizing an amidation reaction between carboxyl group modified silver nanoparticles and CDs. N-(3-dimethylamlnopropy)-N'-ethylcarbodllmide hydrochloride (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were added to activate the carboxyl group on the surface of Ag NPs, which could connect then react with the amino group of CDs. This method provides the formation of individual CDs on the surface of Ag NPs. As a result of the introduction of Ag NPs, quenching of CD fluorescence occurred. Consequently, typical D and G bands of CDs could be observed in the Raman spectrum of Ag NPs/CDs. SERS studies of p-aminothiophenol (PATP) molecules on the Ag NPs/CDs substrate were performed, and the CT between the substrates and PATP molecules was explored at various excitation wavelengths and excitation energies. In addition, Ag NPs/CDs were also used as a nanocatalyst in the oxidation of TMB by H2O2. Thus, a SERS technique was developed for the in situ monitoring of the peroxidase-like catalytic process involving in the oxidation of TMB, and a concentration of H2O2 as low as 10−8 could also be determined.

PATP was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. EDC, NHS and TMB were obtained from Aladdin. Silver nitrate (AgNO3) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. A NaAc-HAc buffer was purchased from Beijing Dingguo Changsheng Biotechnonlogy Co. Ltd. Sodium citrate, H2O2 (30%) and all the solvents were obtained from Beijing Chemical Reagent Co. All the chemicals were used without further purification. CDs were prepared based on a previously reported method from the dehydration between ethylenediamine and citric acid under the condition of certain temperature [17].

Ag NPs were prepared based on the previous report by Meisel et al. [26]. In a typical procedure, 1mM of AgNO3 was added into a three-necked bottle with 200mL of deionized water. Then, 1% sodium citrate (4mL) was added into the above solution under stirring and heated to micro-boiling. Ultimately, colloidal Ag NPs were obtained after 40min, and the synthesized Ag NPs were centrifuged for twice, and then dispersed in 10mL of water for further use.

20mg of EDC and 10mg of NHS were added into the synthesized colloidal silver NPs in 10mL of water. After stirring for about 2h, the reaction solution was centrifuged to remove the redundant reactants and redispersed in 10mL of water. Then, 10mg of CDs were added into the above solution under ultrasonic irradiation. After stirring for about 24h, Ag NPs/CDs were obtained from centrifuging of the reaction solution.

The as-prepared Ag NPs/CDs (or Ag NPs) and PATP aqueous solution with different concentrations (from 1.0×10−5 to 1.0×10−9 M) were mixed for about 30min and then used directly for SERS measurements. In the TMB-H2O2 system for the SERS monitoring of the oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2, 100μL of TMB solution (15mM, DMSO solvent), 200μL of Ag NPs/CDs, 100μL of H2O2 (10−2–10−8 M) were mixed and diluted to 1mL with a NaAc-HAc buffer. The samples were detected by SERS directly after a different reactive time.

A JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (TEM) from JEOL was used to characterize the morphologies of Ag NPs/CDs at 200kV. The UV-via spectra were measured by a UV-3600 UV-Via-NIR spectrophotometer of SHIMADZU. The fluorescence spectra were obtained by a 5301PC fluorescence spectrometer of SHIMADZU. X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data was measured with a Thermo ESCALAB 250 photoelectron spectrometer with Al Kα X-ray radiation. The X-Ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was obtained with a SmartLab (3) diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation. The Raman spectra are collected by a Renishaw-1000 spectrometer (532nm), a LabRAM ARAMIS Smart Raman spectrometer (633nm and 785nm), a Horiba-Jobin Yvon T64000 spectrometer (514.5nm). The Raman spectrometer was calibrated by Raman band at 520.7cm−1 of a Si wafer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and morphology of Ag NPs/CDs
ce:section.ce:para: A typical scheme for the synthetic process of Ag NPs/CDs is illustrated in Fig. 1 . During the preparation of Ag NPs, sodium citrate was used as both a reducing agent and as a stabilizer, to ensure that the carboxyl groups covered the surface of Ag NPs. Additionally, by using citric acid and ethylenediamine, several amino groups were functionalized on the surface of CDs. Subsequently, an amidation reaction was performed to connect the Ag NPs and CDs with the assistance of EDC and NHS [36,37]. An advantage of this approach is that it results in the preparation of uniformly distributed, individual CDs on the surface of Ag NPs. Fig. 2 a shows a TEM image of the as-prepared Ag NPs/CDs material. The diameter of Ag NPs/CDs is found to be∼50nm. From an enlarged TEM image (inset of Fig. 2a), it can be observed that the individual CDs having a diameter of∼5nm are uniformly distributed on the surface of Ag NPs. An XRD pattern was obtained to characterize the crystal structure of Ag NPs/CDs. As shown in Fig. 2b, the peaks corresponding to the (111), (200), (220) and (311) lattice planes demonstrate the highly crystalline phase of silver with a face centered cubic structure. The highly disordered carbon atoms leads to the existence of a wide peak at 25° (0.34nm) [17]. These results indicate the successful formation of the Ag NPs/CDs hybrid.

Fig. 3 a shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of the prepared Ag NPs/CDs, individual CDs and Ag NPs alone. The maximum absorption wavelength of CDs was determined to be 240 and 344nm [17], while that of Ag NPs was observed at∼440nm. On the other hand, the absorption peak of the as-prepared Ag NPs/CDs hybrid is broad and stretches from 320 to 550nm, on the other hand, a broad absorption peak (320–550nm) of the as-prepared Ag NPs/CDs hybrid confirmed that a unique material had formed. The fluorescence spectra of Ag NPs/CDs, Ag NPs and CDs are presented in Fig. 3b. It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of CDs is extraordinarily strong, while Ag NPs and Ag NPs/CDs do not show prominent fluorescence signals. This indicates that, fluorescence quenching takes place in the Ag NPs/CDs system, which may be due to charge transfer between CDs and Ag NPs. It is well known that CDs are photosensitizing electron donors, while Ag NPs are electron acceptors. When the hybrid material is irradiated with photons of a sufficient energy, electron/hole pairs are formed. The electrons produced in this manner can be captured by Ag NPs, which leads to a charge transfer process from CDs to Ag.

The chemical composition at the surface of the prepared Ag NPs/CDs has been determined by XPS measurement. These results are shown in Fig. S1 and Fig. 4 . The wide scan XPS spectrum of Ag NPs/CDs displays clear signals for Ag, C, N and O elements. The binding energies of Ag3d in the XPS spectrum at 368.1eV and 374.1eV can be attributed to Ag3d5/2 and Ag3d3/2, respectively (Fig. 4a). The C1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into three components, with binding energies at 284.6, 285.6 and 287.7eV. The peaks at 284.6 and 285.6eV attributed to CC or CC, and CO or CO, respectively, while the peak at 287.7eV corresponds to CN (Fig. 4b). These observations are consistent with a previous report [17]. The peak of N1s is found at 399.8eV, which is related to the amino group on the surface of CDs (Fig. 4c). The peak at 531.6eV in the O1s spectrum, is associated with the defect sites with low oxygen coordination in CDs. This result further confirms the successful fabrication of the Ag NPs/CDs hybrid.

It is well known that the fluorescence of CDs is so strong that it is difficult to obtain a Raman spectrum of CDs to analyze its chemical structure. However, fluorescence quenching of CDs occurs in the Ag NPs/CDs system, which is beneficial for the SERS measurements. Fig. 5 shows the Raman spectra of Ag NPs/CDs, Ag NPs and CDs alone. It is noteworthy that no obvious peaks appear for Ag NPs and CDs. However, the Raman spectrum of Ag NPs/CDs yields prominent bands due to the carbon materials at 1355cm−1 (D band) and 1603cm−1 (G band) [38,39]. With various excitation wavelengths (514.5, 532 and 785nm), the typical D and G bands can still be seen in the spectrum of Ag NPs/CDs (Fig. S2). This result confirms that the formation Ag NPs/CDs hybrid is responsible for quenching the fluorescence of CDs, and the generated Raman signals of CDs, also confirm the formation of CDs on the surface of Ag NPs.

In this work, the enhancement effect of Ag NPs/CDs as a SERS substrate was also studied. Fig. 6 a shows the SERS spectra of PATP probe molecules with different concentrations. Some of the typical peaks of PATP molecule that can be detected in the SERS spectra occur at 1074 (CS stretching), 1144 (CH bending), 1391 (CH bending+CC stretching) and 1436cm−1 (CH bending+CC stretching) (Table 1 ). Fig. 6b depicts the dependence of the SERS intensities at 1074, 1144, 1391 and 1436cm−1 on the concentration of PATP when Ag NPs/CDs is used as the SERS substrate. The SERS intensity of PATP characteristic peaks decreases with the decrease in PATP concentration. However, the typical peaks of PATP molecules are still observable at a concentration as low as 10−9 M, which demonstrates the remarkable efficiency of Ag NPs/CDs hybrid as a SERS substrate.

It is interesting to note that the SERS spectrum of PATP is to some extent, different from the Raman spectrum of PATP powders. Fig. S3 shows the Raman spectrum of PATP powder. The characteristic peaks at 1092, 1172 and 1598cm−1 are predominant bands in the spectrum, which have been mainly assigned as a1 modes of PATP molecules [28,40]. However, in the SERS spectrum of PATP molecules on the surface of Ag NPs/CDs, peaks at 1144, 1391, 1437 and 1578cm−1, are clearly observed with high intensity [28,40]. These peaks are attributed to the b2 vibrational modes of PATP, and thus, it may be concluded that these vibrational modes are selectively enhanced. A CT mechanism postulates that the electronic interactions take place between Ag NPs/CDs and PATP molecules through the Herzberg-Teller coupling, which can explain the observed SERS enhancement [41].

The CT process between Ag NPs/CDs and PATP molecules was further investigated by studying the SERS spectra of PATP molecules on Ag NPs/CDs and individual Ag NPs with various excitation wavelengths at 514.5 (a, a’), 532 (b, b’), 633 (c, c’) and 785nm (d, d’), as demonstrated in Fig. 7 . The SERS signals of PATP molecules were similar under these excitation wavelengths for both Ag NPs/CDs and individual Ag NPs, which demonstrates that the irradiated wavelength does not influence the adsorption orientation of PATP molecules [41]. Compared with the peak positions of the b2 vibrational modes of PATP molecules on the surface of Ag NPs/CDs and Ag NPs, a large proportion of the positions moved slightly by 1–3cm−1 Raman shift. In addition, the intensities of b2 vibrational peaks (1144, 1391, 1437 and 1578cm−1) gradually decrease relative to the intensities of a1 mode (1075cm−1) with increasing of excitation wavelength (or decreasing excitation energy). On the other hand, compared to individual Ag NPs, the D and G bands of CDs appeared in the spectra when Ag NPs/CDs was used as the SERS substrate. As the excitation wavelength changes from 785nm to 633, 532 and 514.5nm, the intensity of the D and G bands becomes stronger. Thus the characteristic peaks of CDs can be easily detected, and the intensities of PATP molecules decrease with increase in the excitation energy. As the SERS enhancement of the PATP molecules and the resonance Raman enhancement of CDs with D and G bands are competitive, the excitation wavelength at 785 and 633nm are away from the CDs absorption wavelength (344nm), while the excitation wavelength at 532 and 514nm are near the absorption of CDs, thus the Raman intensities of CDs increase and PATP molecules decrease with the increase of excitation energy.

Fig. 8 shows a plots of the ratio of peak intensity at 1144 (b2) and 1075 (a1) cm−1 of PATP molecules located on the surface of Ag NPs/CDs and individual Ag NPs versus the excitation energy. The ratio represents the degree of CT between SERS substrates and probe molecules. The CT from individual Ag NPs and PATP molecules becomes larger with the increase of excitation energy. The degree of CT between Ag NPs/CDs and PATP molecules is less than that between individual Ag NPs and PATP molecules. For individual Ag NPs, the relative intensity of b2/a1 increases continuously as the excitation wavelength changes from 785 to 514.5nm. However, the relative intensity of b2/a1 increases as the excitation wavelength varies from 785 to 532nm, and shows nearly no change from 532 to 514.5nm for Ag NPs/CDs. Therefore, the maximum relative intensity of b2/a1 for both individual Ag NPs and Ag NPs/CDs is observed with the excitation wavelength of 514.5nm. It is well known that the CT process between the metal substrate and adsorbates contribute the chemical enhancement to SERS [42,43]. The higher relative intensity of b2/a1 for Ag NPs at excitation wavelength of 514.5nm may be related to the more change of the potential and polarizability of the molecules under the higher excitation energy [44]. For the Ag NPs/CDs, charge redistribution takes place around the interface among Ag NPs, CDs and PATP molecules. With the competition of the CT process between CDs and Ag NPs, the SERS intensities of PATP molecules decrease with the increase of excitation energy.

The relationship between the relative intensity of b2/a1 of the two bands and the power of the laser has also been studied. Fig. 9 a demonstrates the SERS spectra of PATP molecules on the surface of Ag NPs/CDs with 633nm excitation wavelength with a power of 3.59mW. The intensity of the b2 band at 1144cm−1 of PATP increases gradually, whereas that of the a1 band at 1074cm−1 increases more slowly with time from 0 to 550s. Fig. 9b shows the time dependent variation in the relative intensity of b2/a1 at various power levels. It can be seen from Fig. 9b that the relative intensity of b2/a1 increases with time, but does not change as much after 500s. At a power of 3.59mW, the relative intensity is much higher than that at low power modes. The relationship between the relative intensity of b2/a1 and laser power is shown in Fig. 9c. These results indicate that the intensity of SERS signals of PATP molecules are related to the laser power. With the increase of the laser power, the intensities of b2-type bands increase more than those of a1-type bands, leading to the increase of the relative intensity of b2/a1. It is well known that the enhancement of b2-type bands is related to the chemical enhancement mechanism in SERS, which should be associated to the CT resonance process. The similar results have also been observed for PATP molecules adsorbed on the gold surface [45].

It is well known that nanoparticles of noble metals such as Pt, Pd, Au and Ag possess an excellent peroxidase-like activity [46]. In this work, the peroxidase-like activity of Ag NPs/CDs towards the oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2 has been evaluated by SERS technique. Fig. 10 shows the SERS spectra of the oxidized TMB molecules on the surface of Ag NPs/CDs substrate and TMB molecules in the absence of Ag NPs/CDs. Compared with that of TMB molecules in the absence of the catalyst, the SERS spectrum of the oxidized TMB molecules show the characteristic peaks at 1191, 1337, and 1610cm−1, which are attributed to CH3 bending mode, inter-ring CC stretching mode and ring stretching and CH bending modes, respectively [47]. This result indicates that Ag NPs/CDs can be used as not only a nanocatalyst for the oxidation of TMB but also as an efficient SERS substrate for in situ detection of the product during the catalytic process.

The Ag NPs/CDs SERS substrate system with intrinsic peroxidase-like activity can be applied to provide a simple approach towards the determination of H2O2. Since the SERS intensities of the oxidized TMB molecules are dependent on the concentration of H2O2, the latter can be quantitatively detected by monitoring these intensities. Fig. 11 shows the SERS spectra of the oxidized TMB molecules in the presence of H2O2 with different concentrations. The peaks at 1337 and 1610cm−1 could be observed even when the concentration of H2O2 was as low as 10−8 M although the relative intensity of these characteristic peaks was much weaker than that for a high concentration of H2O2. The dependence of the SERS intensity of the peak at 1610cm−1 corresponding to the oxidized TMB molecules on the different concentrations of H2O2 on the surface of Ag NPs/CDs is plotted in Fig. 12 . The figure inset shows a linear variation between the SERS intensity and −log[H2O2] in the range of 10−5–10−8 M H2O2.

In this work, the hybrid material Ag NPs/CDs consisting of well-dispersed CDs on the surface of Ag NPs was prepared by an amidation reaction. The introduction of CDs into Ag NPs reduces their fluorescence and also results in SERS enhancement. The as-prepared Ag NPs/CDs hybrid can also detect PATP probe molecules by SERS at a concentration as low as 10−9 M. The SERS properties of PATP molecules located on the surface of Ag NPs/CDs also show a CT process between Ag NPs/CDs and PATP molecules. The prepared Ag NPs/CDs hybrid can also be used as a nanocatalyst in the oxidation of TMB using H2O2 as an oxidant, simultaneously affording a simple approach for the determination of H2O2, as well. It is anticipated that such a strategy may be extended to the fabrication of other CDs based nanocomposites for various applications in catalysis and biosensing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21273091, 21327803, 21473068, 51373065, 21504029) and the Development Program of the Science and Technology of Jilin Province (20130522137JH).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2017.03.049.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

The photoluminescence mechanism in carbon dots (graphene quantum dots, carbon nanodots, and polymerdots): current state and future perspective

functionalization and bioimaging applications of highly fluorescent carbon nanoparticles

Water-soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots and photocatalyst design
Electrochemically generated fluorescent fullerene[60] nanoparticles as a new and viable bioimaging platform

Versatile surface plasmon resonance of carbon-dot-supported silver nanoparticles in polymer optoelectronic devices
Solar hydrogen production using carbon quantum dots and a molecular nickel catalyst
Ultrahigh-density array of silver nanoclusters for SERS substrate with high sensitivity and excellent reproducibility
Expanding generality of surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy with borrowing SERS activity strategy
Controlled nucleation for the regulation of the particle size in monodisperse gold suspensions
Adsorption and surface-enhanced raman of dyes on silver and gold sols
A unified approach to surface-enhanced raman spectroscopy
Charge transfer resonance Raman process in surface-enhanced Raman scattering from p-aminothiophenol adsorbed on silver: herzberg-teller contribution

Contribution of ZnO to charge-transfer induced surface-enhanced raman scattering in Au/ZnO/PATP assembly

Fabrication of a highly sensitive surface-enhanced Raman scattering substrate for monitoring of the catalytic degradation of organic pollutants

Covalent attaching protein to graphene oxide via diimide-activated amidation

Scanned chemical enhancement of surface-enhanced Raman scattering using a charge-transfer complex

Interfacial charge-transfer effects in semiconductor-molecule-metal structures: influence of contact variation
Surface-enhanced Raman scattering of 4-aminobenzenethiol on gold: the concept of threshold energy in charge transfer enhancement

Quantitative detection of human tumor necrosis factor α by a resonance raman enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay
